content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array of NNN integers [A1,A2,‚Ä¶,AN][A_1, A_2, \dots, A_N][A1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã]. He wants to perform MMM operations; for each iii (1‚â§i‚â§M1 \le i \le M1‚â§i‚â§M), the iii-th operation consists of reversing the subarray [Ali,‚Ä¶,Ari][A_{l_i}, \dots, A_{r_i}][Ali‚Äã‚Äã,‚Ä¶,Ari‚Äã‚Äã].

Each operation should be applied exactly once. However, Chef does not remember the order in which they should be applied, so he wants to choose a uniformly random permutation of the MMM operations and apply them in that order.

Let's denote by BBB the resulting array if the operations are unpermuted, i.e. applied in the order 111 through MMM. Find the probability that the resulting array (after Chef applies all operations in a random order) is equal to BBB. Specifically, this probability can be expressed as a fraction P/QP/QP/Q, where PPP and QQQ are coprime positive integers; compute PPP and QQQ.
constraints: 1‚â§li‚â§ri‚â§N1 \le l_i \le r_i \le N1‚â§li‚Äã‚â§ri‚Äã‚â§N for each valid iii
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.
MMM lines follow. For each valid iii, the iii-th of these lines contains two space-separated integers lil_ili‚Äã and rir_iri‚Äã.
content.outputFormat: Print a single line containing two integers PPP and QQQ, separated by a slash (the character '/').

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: Each order of the operations results in the same array B=[1,2,1]B = [1, 2, 1]B=[1,2,1].
